concepts.title: Newton’s Shell Theorem
concepts.subject: AP Physics C: Mechanics
concepts.summary:
# Newton’s Shell Theorem — AP Physics C: Mechanics

Newton’s shell theorem is included in the gravitation unit of AP Physics C: Mechanics. Sources: [College Board AP Physics C: Mechanics CED](https://apcentral.collegeboard.org/media/pdf/ap-physics-c-mechanics-course-and-exam-description.pdf) and [MIT OpenCourseWare](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/8-02t-electricity-and-magnetism-spring-2005/resources/16ff64d64187374a61c28a1f9f16f348_chapte4gauss_law/).

## 1. The theorem

Consider a uniform, thin spherical shell with mass $M$ and radius $R$. A test object of mass $m$ is located a distance $r$ from the center.

### Outside the shell: $r>R$

The shell produces the same gravitational force as a point mass $M$ located at its center:

$$
\vec F_g=-\frac{GMm}{r^2}\hat{\mathbf r}
$$

and

$$
\vec g=-\frac{GM}{r^2}\hat{\mathbf r}.
$$

The negative sign means that the field and force point toward the center.

### Inside the shell: $r<R$

The net gravitational force and field are zero:

$$
\boxed{\vec F_g=0}, \qquad \boxed{\vec g=0}.
$$

This is true at every point inside a uniform spherical shell—not only at the center.

Although the nearer side of the shell exerts a stronger force per unit mass, the more distant side contains a larger corresponding area. When all the force vectors are added, they cancel exactly.

Therefore,

$$
\boxed{
g(r)=
\begin{cases}
0, & r<R,\\[4pt]
\dfrac{GM}{r^2}, & r>R.
\end{cases}}
$$

For an ideal infinitely thin shell, the field changes discontinuously at the surface, so the two one-sided results are normally stated separately.

## 2. Gravitational potential

Taking gravitational potential to be zero at infinity,

$$
V(\infty)=0.
$$

Outside the shell:

$$
V(r)=-\frac{GM}{r},\qquad r\ge R.
$$

Inside the shell:

$$
\boxed{V(r)=-\frac{GM}{R}},\qquad r<R.
$$

Thus, the gravitational field inside the shell is zero, but the gravitational potential is not zero. It is a constant negative value.

For a test mass $m$, $U=mV$, so

$$
U(r)=
\begin{cases}
-\dfrac{GMm}{R}, & r<R,\\[6pt]
-\dfrac{GMm}{r}, & r\ge R.
\end{cases}
$$

Remember:

$$
\vec g=-\nabla V.
$$

A zero gravitational field means that the potential has zero spatial slope, not that the potential itself must be zero.

## Extension to spherically symmetric objects

A spherically symmetric object can be divided into many thin concentric shells. At a distance $r$ from the center:

- Every shell with radius smaller than $r$ acts as though its mass were concentrated at the center.
- Every shell with radius larger than $r$ produces zero net gravitational field at that point.

Therefore,

$$
\boxed{g(r)=\frac{GM_{\text{enc}}(r)}{r^2}},
$$

where $M_{\text{enc}}(r)$ is the mass enclosed within radius $r$.

This result can only be applied directly when the mass distribution is spherically symmetric.

## Uniform solid sphere

Consider a uniform solid sphere with total mass $M$ and radius $R$. Its density is

$$
\rho=\frac{M}{\frac43\pi R^3}.
$$

For a point inside the sphere, $r<R$, the enclosed mass is

$$
M_{\text{enc}}
=\rho\left(\frac43\pi r^3\right)
=M\frac{r^3}{R^3}.
$$

Therefore,

$$
g(r)=\frac{GM_{\text{enc}}}{r^2}
=\boxed{\frac{GM}{R^3}r}.
$$

The complete result is

$$
\boxed{
g(r)=
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{GM}{R^3}r, & r<R,\\[6pt]
\dfrac{GM}{r^2}, & r\ge R.
\end{cases}}
$$

Inside a uniform solid sphere, $g$ increases linearly with $r$. Outside the sphere, it decreases according to $1/r^2$.

The gravitational field is:

- zero at the center;
- largest at the surface;
- proportional to $r$ inside;
- proportional to $1/r^2$ outside.

## Key AP Physics points

1. **Check for spherical symmetry first.** The shell theorem cannot be directly applied to a hemisphere, cube, uneven shell, or off-center mass distribution.
2. **Compare $r$ with the object’s radius.** Determine whether the point is inside, outside, or within the material of a thick shell.
3. **Distinguish a thin shell from a solid sphere.** A thin shell has $g=0$ everywhere inside. A uniform solid sphere has $g=(GM/R^3)r$ inside.
4. **Use enclosed mass inside a solid sphere.** Inside the object, $M_{\text{enc}}\ne M$.
5. **Outer shells cancel.** They still exert gravitational forces individually, but their vector sum is zero.
6. **Zero field does not mean zero potential.** Inside a thin shell, $g=0$ while $V=-GM/R$.
7. **Use superposition for multiple shells.** Calculate the contribution from each shell and add the fields or potentials.

## Common mistakes

| Mistake | Correct idea |
| --- | --- |
| Gravity is zero only at the center of a shell | It is zero everywhere inside a uniform shell |
| The object is attracted toward the nearer side | Contributions from the entire shell cancel |
| Every spherical object has zero gravity inside | This is only true inside a hollow uniform shell |
| Using $GM/r^2$ with total $M$ inside a solid sphere | Use the enclosed mass $M_{\text{enc}}$ |
| Assuming $g=0$ means $V=0$ | The potential inside is constant but generally nonzero |
| Measuring $r$ from the surface | $r$ is measured from the center |
| Applying the theorem to any round-looking object | The mass distribution must be spherically symmetric |
| Saying outer shells exert no force | They exert forces whose vector sum is zero |
| Writing gravitational-force magnitude as negative | Magnitude is positive; the sign represents direction |

## Examples

### Example 1: A point inside a thin shell

A uniform thin shell has mass $M=9.0\times10^{10}\ \mathrm{kg}$ and radius $R=4.0\ \mathrm m$. A $2.0\ \mathrm{kg}$ object is located $2.0\ \mathrm m$ from the center.

Since $r<R$, the object is inside the shell. Therefore,

$$
\boxed{F_g=0}.
$$

The object does not need to be at the center for the force to be zero.

If the object is moved to $r=10.0\ \mathrm m$, it is outside the shell:

$$
F_g=\frac{GMm}{r^2}
=\frac{(6.67\times10^{-11})(9.0\times10^{10})(2.0)}{(10.0)^2}
\approx\boxed{0.120\ \mathrm N}
$$

toward the center.

### Example 2: Inside a uniform solid sphere

A uniform solid sphere has mass $M$ and radius $R$. Find the gravitational field at $r=R/2$.

The enclosed mass is

$$
M_{\text{enc}}
=M\left(\frac{R/2}{R}\right)^3
=\frac{M}{8}.
$$

Therefore,

$$
g=\frac{G(M/8)}{(R/2)^2}
=\frac{GM/8}{R^2/4}
=\boxed{\frac{GM}{2R^2}}.
$$

Because $g_{\text{surface}}=GM/R^2$,

$$
\boxed{g(R/2)=\frac12g_{\text{surface}}}.
$$

A common mistake is to predict four times the surface field because the distance is halved. That reasoning fails because the enclosed mass also decreases.

### Example 3: A uniform thick spherical shell

A thick shell has inner radius $a$, outer radius $b$, and uniform density $\rho$. Find the gravitational field within the material, where $a<r<b$.

The enclosed material has volume and mass

$$
V_{\text{enc}}=\frac43\pi(r^3-a^3),
$$

$$
M_{\text{enc}}=\frac43\pi\rho(r^3-a^3).
$$

Thus,

$$
\boxed{
g(r)=\frac{4\pi G\rho}{3}
\left(r-\frac{a^3}{r^2}\right)}.
$$

The complete piecewise result is

$$
g(r)=
\begin{cases}
0, & r<a,\\[6pt]
\dfrac{4\pi G\rho}{3}\left(r-\dfrac{a^3}{r^2}\right), & a<r<b,\\[10pt]
\dfrac{GM}{r^2}, & r>b.
\end{cases}
$$

### Example 4: Work inside a shell

A mass $m$ moves between two locations inside a uniform thin shell of mass $M$ and radius $R$.

Because $\vec g=0$ everywhere inside the shell, $\vec F_g=0$. The potential energy is constant:

$$
U=-\frac{GMm}{R}.
$$

Therefore, $\Delta U=0$ and $W_g=-\Delta U$, so

$$
\boxed{W_g=0}.
$$

## One-sentence summary

> A uniform spherical shell acts like a point mass outside it and produces zero net gravitational field inside it; inside any spherically symmetric solid object, use only the enclosed mass.

The most important working equation is

$$
\boxed{g(r)=\frac{GM_{\text{enc}}(r)}{r^2}},
$$

provided that the mass distribution is spherically symmetric.

concepts.title: Gauss’s law
concepts.subject: AP Physics C: E&M
concepts.summary:
# Gauss’s Law — AP Physics C: Electricity and Magnetism

Gauss’s law is included in the electric-fields unit of AP Physics C: Electricity and Magnetism. It connects the total electric flux through a closed surface with the net electric charge enclosed by that surface.

## 1. Electric flux

Electric flux measures the amount of electric field passing through a surface.

For a small surface element, the area vector is defined as

$d\vec{A}=\hat{n},dA$

The vector $d\vec{A}$ is perpendicular to the surface. For a closed surface, it always points outward.

For a flat surface of area $A$ placed in a uniform electric field,

$\Phi_E=\vec{E}\cdot\vec{A}=EA\cos\theta$

where $\theta$ is the angle between the electric-field vector $\vec{E}$ and the area vector $\vec{A}$.

For a general open surface,

$\Phi_E=\int_S\vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A}$

For a closed surface,

$\Phi_E=\oint_S\vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A}$

The circle on the integral sign indicates that the surface is closed.

For each part of a closed surface:

- the flux is positive when the electric field points outward;
- the flux is negative when the electric field points inward;
- the flux is zero when the electric field is tangent to the surface.

The SI unit of electric flux is

$\mathrm{N,m^2/C}$

Electric flux is a scalar quantity, even though it is calculated using the electric-field vector.

## 2. Gauss’s law

Gauss’s law states that the total electric flux through any closed surface equals the net charge enclosed by that surface divided by the permittivity of free space:

$\oint_S\vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A}=\frac{Q_{\mathrm{enc}}}{\varepsilon_0}$

Here,

- $Q_{\mathrm{enc}}$ is the algebraic sum of all charges inside the Gaussian surface;
- $\varepsilon_0$ is the permittivity of free space.

Its approximate value is

$\varepsilon_0\approx8.85\times10^{-12}\ \mathrm{C^2/(N,m^2)}$

The enclosed charge may be calculated from linear, surface, or volume charge density:

$Q=\int\lambda,d\ell,\qquad Q=\int\sigma,dA,\qquad Q=\int\rho,dV$

Here,

- $\lambda$ is linear charge density, measured in $\mathrm{C/m}$;
- $\sigma$ is surface charge density, measured in $\mathrm{C/m^2}$;
- $\rho$ is volume charge density, measured in $\mathrm{C/m^3}$.

Gauss’s law is always valid for every closed surface, regardless of the shape of the surface or the arrangement of charge.

However, it is only easy to use for calculating the electric field when the charge distribution has sufficient symmetry.

### Differential form

Gauss’s law can also be written in differential form:

$\nabla\cdot\vec{E}=\frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_0}$

This equation means that electric charge is the source of electric field.

Positive charge acts as a source from which electric-field lines emerge, while negative charge acts as a sink toward which electric-field lines converge.

The differential form is part of Maxwell’s equations, but AP Physics C problems usually use the integral form.

## 3. What Gauss’s law does and does not say

Gauss’s law relates the **net electric flux** through a closed surface to the **net enclosed charge**.

It does not say that the electric field at each point depends only on the enclosed charge.

Charges outside a Gaussian surface may affect the electric field at every point on the surface. However, their total contribution to the net flux through the entire closed surface is zero.

Therefore,

$Q_{\mathrm{enc}}=0\quad\Longrightarrow\quad\Phi_E=0$

However, this does not necessarily mean

$\vec{E}=0$

For example, suppose a closed box is placed in a uniform external electric field. The field enters one side of the box and leaves the opposite side. The incoming and outgoing fluxes cancel, so the net flux is zero even though the electric field is not zero.

Similarly,

$\Phi_E=0$

does not necessarily mean that no charges are inside the surface.

There may be both positive and negative charges inside whose algebraic sum is zero.

Therefore:

- zero enclosed charge means zero net flux;
- zero net flux does not necessarily mean zero electric field;
- zero net flux does not necessarily mean no charge is present.

## 4. Choosing a Gaussian surface

A Gaussian surface is an imaginary three-dimensional closed surface used to apply Gauss’s law.

It does not have to correspond to the surface of a real physical object.

A useful Gaussian surface should satisfy as many of the following conditions as possible:

1. The electric field has the same magnitude over a large part of the surface.
2. The electric field is parallel or antiparallel to the area vector on surfaces where the flux is nonzero.
3. The electric field is perpendicular to the area vector on surfaces where the flux is zero.
4. The enclosed charge can be calculated easily.
5. The Gaussian surface follows the symmetry of the charge distribution.

The three most important types of symmetry are:

| Charge distribution | Symmetry | Useful Gaussian surface |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Point charge, spherical shell, uniform sphere | Spherical symmetry | Sphere |
| Infinite line charge, infinite cylinder | Cylindrical symmetry | Coaxial cylinder |
| Infinite sheet, infinite slab | Planar symmetry | Pillbox |

A Gaussian surface may always be drawn, but Gauss’s law cannot always be simplified enough to solve for $E$.

For example, Gauss’s law is valid for a cube surrounding a point charge. However, if the charge is not positioned symmetrically, the electric field is not constant over the faces of the cube. Therefore, the flux integral cannot simply be written as $EA$.

## 5. Spherical symmetry

For a spherically symmetric charge distribution, the electric field must point radially.

Its magnitude depends only on the distance $r$ from the center.

Choose a spherical Gaussian surface of radius $r$.

Because $\vec{E}$ is parallel to the outward area vector at every point,

$\oint_S\vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A}=E(r)\oint_S dA$

The surface area of a sphere is

$4\pi r^2$

Therefore,

$\oint_S\vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A}=E(r)(4\pi r^2)$

Using Gauss’s law,

$E(r)(4\pi r^2)=\frac{Q_{\mathrm{enc}}(r)}{\varepsilon_0}$

Thus,

$\vec{E}(r)=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{Q_{\mathrm{enc}}(r)}{r^2}\hat{r}$

This equation may be used directly only when the charge distribution is spherically symmetric.

The sign of the enclosed charge determines the direction:

- if $Q_{\mathrm{enc}}>0$, the field points outward;
- if $Q_{\mathrm{enc}}<0$, the field points inward.

### Uniform thin spherical shell

Consider a uniformly charged thin spherical shell with total charge $Q$ and radius $R$.

#### Outside the shell: $r>R$

The Gaussian surface encloses the entire charge:

$Q_{\mathrm{enc}}=Q$

Therefore,

$\vec{E}(r)=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{Q}{r^2}\hat{r}$

The shell produces the same external electric field as a point charge $Q$ placed at its center.

#### Inside the shell: $r<R$

The Gaussian surface encloses no charge:

$Q_{\mathrm{enc}}=0$

Because of spherical symmetry, the electric field has the same magnitude everywhere on the Gaussian surface.

Gauss’s law gives

$E(4\pi r^2)=0$

Therefore,

$\vec{E}=0$

The complete result is

$\vec{E}(r)=\begin{cases}0,&r<R,\[6pt]\dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\dfrac{Q}{r^2}\hat{r},&r>R.\end{cases}$

The electric field is zero at every point inside a uniformly charged spherical shell, not only at the center.

For an ideal infinitely thin charged shell, the normal component of the electric field changes discontinuously at the surface:

$E_{\perp,\mathrm{out}}-E_{\perp,\mathrm{in}}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}$

For the spherical shell,

$\sigma=\frac{Q}{4\pi R^2}$

Since the electric field inside is zero,

$E_{\mathrm{in}}=0$

so immediately outside the shell,

$E_{\mathrm{out}}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}=\frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0R^2}$

### Uniform solid insulating sphere

Consider a uniformly charged insulating sphere with radius $R$ and total charge $Q$.

Its volume charge density is

$\rho=\frac{Q}{\frac{4}{3}\pi R^3}=\frac{3Q}{4\pi R^3}$

For a point inside the sphere, where $r<R$, the enclosed charge is

$Q_{\mathrm{enc}}(r)=\rho\left(\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3\right)$

Substituting the density,

$Q_{\mathrm{enc}}(r)=Q\frac{r^3}{R^3}$

Using Gauss’s law,

$E(r)=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{Q_{\mathrm{enc}}(r)}{r^2}$

Therefore,

$E(r)=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{Qr}{R^3}$

The complete electric field is

$\vec{E}(r)=\begin{cases}\dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\dfrac{Qr}{R^3}\hat{r},&r<R,\[8pt]\dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\dfrac{Q}{r^2}\hat{r},&r\geq R.\end{cases}$

For a uniform solid insulating sphere, the electric field is:

- zero at the center;
- proportional to $r$ inside the sphere;
- largest at the surface;
- proportional to $1/r^2$ outside the sphere.

A solid insulating sphere is different from a conducting sphere.

An insulator may contain charge throughout its volume, so the electric field inside it may be nonzero.

### Uniform thick spherical shell

Consider a uniformly charged nonconducting thick spherical shell with inner radius $a$, outer radius $b$, and volume charge density $\rho$.

#### Region 1: $r<a$

The Gaussian surface encloses no charge:

$Q_{\mathrm{enc}}=0$

Therefore,

$\vec{E}=0$

#### Region 2: $a<r<b$

The enclosed charged volume is

$V_{\mathrm{enc}}=\frac{4}{3}\pi(r^3-a^3)$

Thus,

$Q_{\mathrm{enc}}=\frac{4}{3}\pi\rho(r^3-a^3)$

Gauss’s law gives

$E(4\pi r^2)=\frac{\frac{4}{3}\pi\rho(r^3-a^3)}{\varepsilon_0}$

Therefore,

$\vec{E}(r)=\frac{\rho}{3\varepsilon_0}\left(r-\frac{a^3}{r^2}\right)\hat{r}$

#### Region 3: $r>b$

The total charge of the shell is

$Q=\frac{4}{3}\pi\rho(b^3-a^3)$

Therefore,

$\vec{E}(r)=\frac{\rho(b^3-a^3)}{3\varepsilon_0r^2}\hat{r}$

The complete result is

$\vec{E}(r)=\begin{cases}0,&r<a,\[8pt]\dfrac{\rho}{3\varepsilon_0}\left(r-\dfrac{a^3}{r^2}\right)\hat{r},&a<r<b,\[10pt]\dfrac{\rho(b^3-a^3)}{3\varepsilon_0r^2}\hat{r},&r>b.\end{cases}$

## 6. Cylindrical symmetry

Cylindrical symmetry applies to idealized infinitely long line charges and infinitely long uniformly charged cylinders.

The electric field points radially away from or toward the central axis.

Its magnitude depends only on the perpendicular distance $r$ from the axis.

Choose a cylindrical Gaussian surface with radius $r$ and length $L$.

The curved surface area is

$A_{\mathrm{curved}}=2\pi rL$

The electric field is perpendicular to the curved surface and parallel to the two end caps.

Therefore, the flux through the end caps is zero.

Only the curved surface contributes to the total flux:

$\Phi_E=E(2\pi rL)$

### Infinite line of charge

Consider an infinitely long line with uniform linear charge density $\lambda$.

The enclosed charge is

$Q_{\mathrm{enc}}=\lambda L$

Gauss’s law gives

$E(2\pi rL)=\frac{\lambda L}{\varepsilon_0}$

The arbitrary length $L$ cancels:

$\vec{E}(r)=\frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_0r}\hat{r}$

The electric field of an infinite line charge decreases according to

$E\propto\frac{1}{r}$

This differs from the field of a point charge, which decreases according to $1/r^2$.

The difference occurs because the flux of a line charge spreads over a cylindrical area proportional to $r$, rather than a spherical area proportional to $r^2$.

### Uniform solid insulating cylinder

Consider an infinitely long insulating cylinder with radius $R$ and uniform volume charge density $\rho$.

#### Inside the cylinder: $r<R$

The enclosed charge is

$Q_{\mathrm{enc}}=\rho\pi r^2L$

Gauss’s law gives

$E(2\pi rL)=\frac{\rho\pi r^2L}{\varepsilon_0}$

Therefore,

$\vec{E}(r)=\frac{\rho r}{2\varepsilon_0}\hat{r}$

#### Outside the cylinder: $r\geq R$

The enclosed charge is

$Q_{\mathrm{enc}}=\rho\pi R^2L$

Therefore,

$E(2\pi rL)=\frac{\rho\pi R^2L}{\varepsilon_0}$

Thus,

$\vec{E}(r)=\frac{\rho R^2}{2\varepsilon_0r}\hat{r}$

The complete result is

$\vec{E}(r)=\begin{cases}\dfrac{\rho r}{2\varepsilon_0}\hat{r},&r<R,\[8pt]\dfrac{\rho R^2}{2\varepsilon_0r}\hat{r},&r\geq R.\end{cases}$

Inside the uniformly charged solid cylinder, the field increases linearly with $r$.

Outside the cylinder, it decreases according to $1/r$.

## 7. Planar symmetry

Planar symmetry applies to idealized infinite sheets and infinite slabs of charge.

The electric field must be perpendicular to the plane.

Its magnitude cannot depend on position parallel to the plane because every point on an infinite uniform plane is equivalent.

A short cylindrical Gaussian surface called a pillbox is normally used.

### Infinite nonconducting sheet

Consider an infinite nonconducting sheet with uniform surface charge density $\sigma$.

The electric field has equal magnitude on both sides of the sheet.

The curved side of the pillbox contributes no flux because the electric field is parallel to that surface.

Each flat face contributes $EA$, so the total flux is

$\Phi_E=2EA$

The enclosed charge is

$Q_{\mathrm{enc}}=\sigma A$

Gauss’s law gives

$2EA=\frac{\sigma A}{\varepsilon_0}$

Therefore,

$E=\frac{|\sigma|}{2\varepsilon_0}$

For a positively charged sheet, the electric field points away from the sheet.

For a negatively charged sheet, it points toward the sheet.

If the sheet lies in the plane $x=0$, the field may be written as

$\vec{E}(x)=\frac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_0}\operatorname{sgn}(x)\hat{x}$

The electric field of an ideal infinite sheet does not decrease with distance.

This occurs because increasing the size of the Gaussian pillbox increases both the enclosed charge and the flux area by the same factor.

### Surface of a conductor

For a conductor in electrostatic equilibrium,

$\vec{E}_{\mathrm{inside}}=0$

Use a thin Gaussian pillbox crossing the surface of the conductor.

Gauss’s law gives the boundary condition

$E_{\perp,\mathrm{out}}-E_{\perp,\mathrm{in}}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}$

Because the field inside the conductor is zero,

$E_{\perp,\mathrm{in}}=0$

Therefore, immediately outside the conductor,

$\vec{E}_{\mathrm{out}}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}\hat{n}$

This is different from the field of an isolated infinite nonconducting sheet:

$E_{\mathrm{sheet}}=\frac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_0}$

For the isolated nonconducting sheet, equal electric fields exist on both sides.

For the conductor, the electric field inside the conducting material must be zero, so the entire flux appears on the outside.

### Two oppositely charged infinite sheets

Consider two infinite sheets with surface charge densities $+\sigma$ and $-\sigma$.

Each individual sheet produces a field of magnitude

$\frac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_0}$

Using superposition:

- outside the two sheets, the fields point in opposite directions and cancel;
- between the sheets, the fields point in the same direction and add.

Therefore,

$E=\begin{cases}0,&\text{outside the sheets},\[6pt]\dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0},&\text{between the sheets}.\end{cases}$

The electric field between the sheets points from the positive sheet toward the negative sheet.

This is the idealized electric field between two large parallel capacitor plates when edge effects are neglected.

### Uniform infinite slab

Consider an infinite slab extending from $x=-a$ to $x=a$, with uniform volume charge density $\rho$.

#### Inside the slab: $|x|<a$

Choose a symmetric pillbox extending from $-x$ to $+x$.

The enclosed volume is

$V_{\mathrm{enc}}=2xA$

Therefore,

$Q_{\mathrm{enc}}=2\rho xA$

The total flux through the two faces is

$\Phi_E=2EA$

Gauss’s law gives

$2EA=\frac{2\rho xA}{\varepsilon_0}$

Therefore,

$\vec{E}(x)=\frac{\rho x}{\varepsilon_0}\hat{x}$

#### Outside the slab: $|x|\geq a$

The Gaussian surface encloses the full thickness $2a$:

$Q_{\mathrm{enc}}=2\rho aA$

Therefore,

$2EA=\frac{2\rho aA}{\varepsilon_0}$

Thus,

$E=\frac{\rho a}{\varepsilon_0}$

The complete result is

$\vec{E}(x)=\begin{cases}\dfrac{\rho x}{\varepsilon_0}\hat{x},&|x|<a,\[8pt]\dfrac{\rho a}{\varepsilon_0}\operatorname{sgn}(x)\hat{x},&|x|\geq a.\end{cases}$

Inside the slab, the electric field increases linearly with the distance from the center.

Outside the slab, the field has constant magnitude.

## 8. Conductors in electrostatic equilibrium

A conductor is in electrostatic equilibrium when its free charges are no longer moving macroscopically.

The main properties of a conductor in electrostatic equilibrium are:

1. The electric field inside the conducting material is zero.
2. Any excess net charge lies on the surface.
3. The entire conductor is at the same electric potential.
4. The electric field immediately outside the conductor is perpendicular to the surface.
5. The tangential component of the electric field at the surface is zero.
6. The electric field immediately outside is related to the local surface charge density by $E_\perp=\sigma/\varepsilon_0$.

The electric field inside a conductor must be zero because free charges would otherwise accelerate.

If the charges were moving, the conductor would not be in electrostatic equilibrium.

### Charge inside a conducting cavity

Suppose a point charge $q$ is placed inside a cavity within a conductor.

Choose a Gaussian surface entirely within the conducting material and surrounding the cavity.

Because the electric field inside the conducting material is zero,

$\oint\vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A}=0$

Therefore,

$Q_{\mathrm{enc}}=0$

The enclosed charge consists of the charge $q$ and the induced charge $Q_{\mathrm{inner}}$ on the inner wall:

$q+Q_{\mathrm{inner}}=0$

Thus,

$Q_{\mathrm{inner}}=-q$

If the conductor originally has total charge $Q_{\mathrm{conductor}}$, then the charge remaining on the outer surface is

$Q_{\mathrm{outer}}=Q_{\mathrm{conductor}}+q$

The total induced charge on the cavity wall is always $-q$.

However, the charge density on the cavity wall is not necessarily uniform unless the geometry and charge placement have sufficient symmetry.

## 9. Electric potential connection

Electric field and electric potential are related by

$\vec{E}=-\nabla V$

For a one-dimensional radial situation,

$E_r=-\frac{dV}{dr}$

A zero electric field means that the electric potential is constant in space.

It does not mean that the electric potential must equal zero.

### Potential of a uniformly charged spherical shell

Take the electric potential to be zero at infinity:

$V(\infty)=0$

For a uniformly charged spherical shell with total charge $Q$ and radius $R$:

#### Outside the shell: $r\geq R$

The potential is the same as that of a point charge:

$V(r)=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{Q}{r}$

#### Inside the shell: $r<R$

The electric field is zero, so the potential is constant.

The constant value must equal the potential at the surface:

$V(r)=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{Q}{R}$

Therefore,

$V(r)=\begin{cases}\dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\dfrac{Q}{R},&r<R,\[10pt]\dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\dfrac{Q}{r},&r\geq R.\end{cases}$

Inside the shell,

$\vec{E}=0$

but

$V=\frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0R}$

is generally not zero.

The potential is continuous at the surface, even though the electric field of an ideal thin shell changes discontinuously there.

For a test charge $q$,

$U=qV$

If the test charge moves between two points inside the shell,

$\Delta V=0$

Therefore,

$\Delta U=q\Delta V=0$

Since

$W_{\mathrm{electric}}=-\Delta U$

the electric force does no net work:

$W_{\mathrm{electric}}=0$

## 10. General Gauss’s-law calculation procedure

### Step 1: Identify the symmetry

Determine whether the charge distribution has:

- spherical symmetry;
- cylindrical symmetry;
- planar symmetry;
- insufficient symmetry.

Do not choose a Gaussian surface simply because the physical object looks round or regular.

The charge distribution itself must have the required symmetry.

### Step 2: Choose the Gaussian surface

Choose a closed surface on which:

- $E$ is constant wherever the flux is nonzero;
- $\vec{E}$ is parallel or antiparallel to $d\vec{A}$;
- or $\vec{E}$ is perpendicular to $d\vec{A}$, so that part contributes zero flux.

### Step 3: Calculate the electric flux

Start with

$\oint_S\vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A}$

Use symmetry to simplify it.

For spherical symmetry,

$\oint_S\vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A}=E(4\pi r^2)$

For cylindrical symmetry,

$\oint_S\vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A}=E(2\pi rL)$

For a pillbox crossing an infinite nonconducting sheet,

$\oint_S\vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A}=2EA$

### Step 4: Find the enclosed charge

Calculate only the charge inside the chosen Gaussian surface.

For a volume distribution,

$Q_{\mathrm{enc}}=\int\rho,dV$

For a surface distribution,

$Q_{\mathrm{enc}}=\int\sigma,dA$

For a line distribution,

$Q_{\mathrm{enc}}=\int\lambda,d\ell$

### Step 5: Apply Gauss’s law

Use

$\oint_S\vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A}=\frac{Q_{\mathrm{enc}}}{\varepsilon_0}$

### Step 6: State the direction

The calculation often gives only the magnitude.

The final answer should state whether the field points:

- outward or inward;
- away from or toward an axis;
- above or below a plane;
- from positive charge toward negative charge.

## 11. Key AP Physics points

### Gauss’s law is always valid but not always useful for finding $E$

Gauss’s law applies to every closed surface.

However, without enough symmetry, the electric-field magnitude cannot be taken outside the flux integral.

### Use net enclosed charge

Only charges inside the Gaussian surface contribute to $Q_{\mathrm{enc}}$.

Charges outside the surface are not included in the right side of Gauss’s law.

### External charges may still affect the electric field

External charges may create a nonzero electric field at points on the Gaussian surface.

Their net contribution to the total flux is zero, but their local field is not necessarily zero.

### The Gaussian surface must be closed

Gauss’s law cannot be directly applied to a single disk, one face of a cube, or another open surface.

### The Gaussian surface is imaginary

It does not need to coincide with the surface of a physical object.

### Flux is not the same as electric field

Electric flux depends on:

- electric-field magnitude;
- surface area;
- orientation;
- whether the field points inward or outward.

### Zero net flux does not mean zero electric field

A nonzero field may enter and leave a closed surface with equal total flux.

### Zero net flux does not mean there are no enclosed charges

The enclosed positive and negative charges may add to zero.

### Use enclosed charge inside an insulator

Inside a uniformly charged solid object, use only the charge within the Gaussian radius.

Do not automatically use the total charge of the object.

### The field inside a conductor is zero only in electrostatic equilibrium

A conductor carrying a changing current or experiencing time-dependent electromagnetic effects may not satisfy the simple electrostatic result.

### Distinguish conductors from insulators

A conductor allows charges to move freely and places excess charge on its surface in electrostatic equilibrium.

An insulator may retain charge throughout its volume.

### Distinguish an infinite sheet from a conducting surface

For an isolated infinite nonconducting sheet,

$E=\frac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_0}$

Immediately outside a conductor,

$E=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}$

## 12. Common mistakes

| Mistake | Correct idea |
| --- | --- |
| Gauss’s law works only for spherical objects | It is valid for every closed surface |
| If $Q_{\mathrm{enc}}=0$, then $\vec{E}=0$ | Only the net flux must be zero |
| External charges should be included in $Q_{\mathrm{enc}}$ | Include only charges inside the surface |
| External charges have no effect | They can affect the local field but not the net enclosed-charge term |
| Total charge should always be used inside a solid insulator | Use only the enclosed charge |
| A Gaussian surface is a real physical surface | It is an imaginary mathematical surface |
| Gauss’s law can be applied directly to an open surface | The surface must be closed |
| Flux is always $EA$ | In general, use $\vec{E}\cdot\vec{A}=EA\cos\theta$ |
| A symmetric Gaussian surface guarantees that $E$ is constant | The charge distribution must also have symmetry |
| Spherical $r$ is measured from the surface | It is measured from the center |
| Cylindrical $r$ is measured from the end | It is measured perpendicular to the axis |
| The field outside a conductor is $\sigma/(2\varepsilon_0)$ | Immediately outside a conductor, it is $\sigma/\varepsilon_0$ |
| The field inside every charged sphere is zero | This is not true for a charged solid insulator |
| Electric flux is a vector | Electric flux is a scalar |
| Zero field means zero potential | Zero field means constant potential |

## 13. Examples

### Example 1: Point charge inside a closed surface

A point charge $q$ is placed anywhere inside a closed surface.

Gauss’s law gives

$\Phi_E=\oint_S\vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}$

The total flux does not depend on:

- the size of the surface;
- the shape of the surface;
- the position of the charge inside the surface.

However, if the charge is off-center, the flux is not distributed uniformly over the surface.

If the charge is at the exact center of a cube, symmetry requires the flux to be divided equally among the six faces:

$\Phi_{\mathrm{face}}=\frac{1}{6}\Phi_{\mathrm{total}}$

Therefore,

$\Phi_{\mathrm{face}}=\frac{q}{6\varepsilon_0}$

If the charge is not at the center, the total flux is still $q/\varepsilon_0$, but the six faces do not receive equal flux.

### Example 2: Inside a uniform solid sphere

A uniform insulating sphere has radius $R$ and total charge $Q$.

Find the electric field at

$r=\frac{R}{2}$

The enclosed charge is

$Q_{\mathrm{enc}}=Q\left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^3$

Substituting $r=R/2$,

$Q_{\mathrm{enc}}=Q\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^3=\frac{Q}{8}$

Gauss’s law gives

$E=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{Q_{\mathrm{enc}}}{r^2}$

Therefore,

$E\left(\frac{R}{2}\right)=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{Q/8}{(R/2)^2}$

Since

$\left(\frac{R}{2}\right)^2=\frac{R^2}{4}$

we obtain

$E\left(\frac{R}{2}\right)=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{Q}{2R^2}$

The field at the surface is

$E_{\mathrm{surface}}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{Q}{R^2}$

Therefore,

$E\left(\frac{R}{2}\right)=\frac{1}{2}E_{\mathrm{surface}}$

A common mistake is to predict that the field is four times larger because the distance is halved.

That reasoning fails because the enclosed charge also decreases.

### Example 3: Infinite line of charge

An infinitely long wire has uniform linear charge density $\lambda$.

Find the electric field a perpendicular distance $r$ from the wire.

Choose a cylindrical Gaussian surface with radius $r$ and length $L$.

The flux through the end caps is zero.

The flux through the curved surface is

$\Phi_E=E(2\pi rL)$

The enclosed charge is

$Q_{\mathrm{enc}}=\lambda L$

Using Gauss’s law,

$E(2\pi rL)=\frac{\lambda L}{\varepsilon_0}$

Canceling $L$ gives

$\vec{E}(r)=\frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_0r}\hat{r}$

For positive $\lambda$, the field points away from the wire.

For negative $\lambda$, it points toward the wire.

### Example 4: Electric flux with no enclosed charge

A cube is placed in a uniform electric field and contains no charge.

Assume that the field enters one face and leaves the opposite face.

The incoming flux is

$\Phi_{\mathrm{in}}=-EA$

The outgoing flux is

$\Phi_{\mathrm{out}}=+EA$

The field is parallel to the other four faces, so those faces contribute zero flux.

Therefore,

$\Phi_{\mathrm{net}}=-EA+EA=0$

Gauss’s law confirms the result:

$\Phi_{\mathrm{net}}=\frac{Q_{\mathrm{enc}}}{\varepsilon_0}=0$

The electric field is not zero, but the net electric flux is zero.

### Example 5: Uniform thick spherical shell

A uniformly charged insulating thick shell has inner radius $a$, outer radius $b$, and volume charge density $\rho$.

Find the electric field within the material, where

$a<r<b$

The enclosed volume is

$V_{\mathrm{enc}}=\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3-\frac{4}{3}\pi a^3$

Therefore,

$V_{\mathrm{enc}}=\frac{4}{3}\pi(r^3-a^3)$

The enclosed charge is

$Q_{\mathrm{enc}}=\frac{4}{3}\pi\rho(r^3-a^3)$

Gauss’s law gives

$E(4\pi r^2)=\frac{\frac{4}{3}\pi\rho(r^3-a^3)}{\varepsilon_0}$

Therefore,

$\vec{E}(r)=\frac{\rho}{3\varepsilon_0}\left(r-\frac{a^3}{r^2}\right)\hat{r}$

At $r=a$, the field approaches zero.

At $r=b$, the internal expression matches the external field calculated using the shell’s total charge.

## 14. Comparison of the three principal symmetries

| Symmetry | Gaussian area carrying flux | Typical external field dependence |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Spherical | $4\pi r^2$ | $E\propto1/r^2$ |
| Cylindrical | $2\pi rL$ | $E\propto1/r$ |
| Planar | $2A$ | $E$ is independent of distance |

The different distance dependences come from how electric flux spreads through space.

For spherical symmetry, the flux spreads over an area proportional to $r^2$:

$A_{\mathrm{sphere}}=4\pi r^2$

Therefore,

$E\propto\frac{1}{r^2}$

For cylindrical symmetry, the flux spreads over an area proportional to $r$:

$A_{\mathrm{cylinder}}=2\pi rL$

Therefore,

$E\propto\frac{1}{r}$

For an infinite plane, the relevant Gaussian area does not increase with distance from the plane.

Therefore, the field remains constant.

## One-sentence summary

Gauss’s law states that the net electric flux through any closed surface equals the net enclosed charge divided by $\varepsilon_0$; it is most useful for calculating electric fields when spherical, cylindrical, or planar symmetry makes the flux integral easy to evaluate.

The fundamental equation is

$\boxed{\oint_S\vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A}=\frac{Q_{\mathrm{enc}}}{\varepsilon_0}}$

The most important practical principle is:

> Gauss’s law is always valid, but solving directly for the electric field requires sufficient symmetry.

concepts.summary:
# AP Physics C: Electricity and Magnetism — Gauss’s Law Practice FRQ

## Problem: Nonuniformly Charged Insulating Sphere

A solid nonconducting sphere has radius $R$ and is centered at the origin. The sphere contains a positive volume charge density that varies with distance $r$ from the center according to

$\rho(r)=\rho_0\frac{r}{R}$

for $0\leq r\leq R$, where $\rho_0$ is a positive constant.

There is no charge outside the sphere.

Let the electric potential be zero at infinity.

### Part (a)

Using an appropriate Gaussian surface, derive an expression for the charge $Q_{\mathrm{enc}}(r)$ enclosed within a radius $r<R$.

### Part (b)

Derive an expression for the magnitude of the electric field $E(r)$ inside the sphere, where $0<r<R$.

### Part (c)

Determine the total charge $Q$ of the sphere.

### Part (d)

Derive an expression for the magnitude of the electric field outside the sphere, where $r>R$.

### Part (e)

Show that the electric field is continuous at the surface $r=R$.

### Part (f)

On the axes provided, sketch the magnitude of the electric field $E$ as a function of $r$ from $r=0$ to $r=3R$.

Clearly indicate:

- the value of the field at $r=0$;
- the value of the field at $r=R$;
- the behavior of the field for $r<R$;
- the behavior of the field for $r>R$.

### Part (g)

Determine the electric potential at the surface of the sphere.

### Part (h)

Derive an expression for the electric potential $V(r)$ inside the sphere, where $0\leq r<R$.

# Solution

## Part (a): Enclosed charge

Because the charge distribution is spherically symmetric, choose a spherical Gaussian surface of radius $r<R$.

The volume charge density is

$\rho(r')=\rho_0\frac{r'}{R}$

A thin spherical shell of radius $r'$ and thickness $dr'$ has volume

$dV=4\pi r'^2,dr'$

The charge contained in the thin shell is

$dQ=\rho(r'),dV$

Substituting the charge density,

$dQ=\left(\rho_0\frac{r'}{R}\right)\left(4\pi r'^2,dr'\right)$

Therefore,

$dQ=\frac{4\pi\rho_0}{R}r'^3,dr'$

The charge enclosed within radius $r$ is

$Q_{\mathrm{enc}}(r)=\int_0^r dQ$

Thus,

$Q_{\mathrm{enc}}(r)=\frac{4\pi\rho_0}{R}\int_0^r r'^3,dr'$

Evaluating the integral,

$Q_{\mathrm{enc}}(r)=\frac{4\pi\rho_0}{R}\left(\frac{r^4}{4}\right)$

Therefore,

$\boxed{Q_{\mathrm{enc}}(r)=\frac{\pi\rho_0r^4}{R}}$

### AP reasoning point

The variable $r'$ is used inside the integral so that it is not confused with the final Gaussian radius $r$.

A common mistake is to write

$Q_{\mathrm{enc}}=\rho(r)\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$

This is incorrect because the charge density is not constant throughout the sphere.

## Part (b): Electric field inside the sphere

Gauss’s law is

$\oint_S\vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A}=\frac{Q_{\mathrm{enc}}}{\varepsilon_0}$

Because of spherical symmetry, the electric field is radial and has the same magnitude everywhere on the spherical Gaussian surface.

Therefore,

$\oint_S\vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A}=E(r)(4\pi r^2)$

Applying Gauss’s law,

$E(r)(4\pi r^2)=\frac{Q_{\mathrm{enc}}(r)}{\varepsilon_0}$

Substitute the result from part (a):

$E(r)(4\pi r^2)=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{\pi\rho_0r^4}{R}\right)$

Solving for $E(r)$,

$E(r)=\frac{\pi\rho_0r^4}{4\pi\varepsilon_0Rr^2}$

Therefore,

$\boxed{E(r)=\frac{\rho_0r^2}{4\varepsilon_0R}}$

Because the charge density is positive, the electric field points radially outward.

The vector form is

$\boxed{\vec{E}(r)=\frac{\rho_0r^2}{4\varepsilon_0R}\hat{r}}$

### Interpretation

Inside the sphere,

$E(r)\propto r^2$

The field increases quadratically rather than linearly because the volume charge density itself increases with $r$.

At the center,

$E(0)=0$

## Part (c): Total charge

The total charge is obtained by setting $r=R$ in the enclosed-charge expression:

$Q=Q_{\mathrm{enc}}(R)$

Therefore,

$Q=\frac{\pi\rho_0R^4}{R}$

Thus,

$\boxed{Q=\pi\rho_0R^3}$

The same result could be found directly from

$Q=\int_0^R\rho(r),4\pi r^2,dr$

## Part (d): Electric field outside the sphere

For $r>R$, a spherical Gaussian surface encloses the entire charge:

$Q_{\mathrm{enc}}=Q=\pi\rho_0R^3$

Gauss’s law gives

$E(r)(4\pi r^2)=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0}$

Substituting the total charge,

$E(r)(4\pi r^2)=\frac{\pi\rho_0R^3}{\varepsilon_0}$

Solving for the electric field,

$E(r)=\frac{\pi\rho_0R^3}{4\pi\varepsilon_0r^2}$

Therefore,

$\boxed{E(r)=\frac{\rho_0R^3}{4\varepsilon_0r^2}}$

The vector form is

$\boxed{\vec{E}(r)=\frac{\rho_0R^3}{4\varepsilon_0r^2}\hat{r}}$

Outside the sphere, the charge distribution acts like a point charge of magnitude $Q$ located at the center.

The external field obeys

$E(r)\propto\frac{1}{r^2}$

## Part (e): Continuity at the surface

Approach the surface from inside:

$E(R^-)=\frac{\rho_0R^2}{4\varepsilon_0R}$

Therefore,

$E(R^-)=\frac{\rho_0R}{4\varepsilon_0}$

Approach the surface from outside:

$E(R^+)=\frac{\rho_0R^3}{4\varepsilon_0R^2}$

Therefore,

$E(R^+)=\frac{\rho_0R}{4\varepsilon_0}$

Thus,

$E(R^-)=E(R^+)$

Therefore, the electric field is continuous at $r=R$:

$\boxed{E(R)=\frac{\rho_0R}{4\varepsilon_0}}$

The field is continuous because there is no infinitely thin surface charge layer at $r=R$.

The volume charge density reaches the finite value

$\rho(R)=\rho_0$

but there is no separate surface charge density $\sigma$ producing a discontinuity.

## Part (f): Electric-field graph

The complete electric-field magnitude is

$E(r)=\begin{cases}\dfrac{\rho_0r^2}{4\varepsilon_0R},&0\leq r<R,\[6pt]\dfrac{\rho_0R^3}{4\varepsilon_0r^2},&r\geq R.\end{cases}$

Important graph features:

- At the center, $E(0)=0$.
- Inside the sphere, $E$ increases as $r^2$.
- At the surface, $E(R)=\dfrac{\rho_0R}{4\varepsilon_0}$.
- The field reaches its maximum value at $r=R$.
- Outside the sphere, $E$ decreases as $1/r^2$.
- The graph is continuous at $r=R$.
- The slope generally changes at $r=R$.

Useful comparison values are:

$E(2R)=\frac{\rho_0R^3}{4\varepsilon_0(2R)^2}=\frac{\rho_0R}{16\varepsilon_0}$

Therefore,

$E(2R)=\frac{1}{4}E(R)$

Similarly,

$E(3R)=\frac{\rho_0R^3}{4\varepsilon_0(3R)^2}=\frac{\rho_0R}{36\varepsilon_0}$

Therefore,

$E(3R)=\frac{1}{9}E(R)$

A correct sketch begins at the origin, curves upward quadratically to a maximum at $r=R$, and then decreases toward zero according to $1/r^2$.

## Part (g): Potential at the surface

Outside the sphere, the potential is the same as that of a point charge:

$V(r)=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{Q}{r}$

At the surface,

$V(R)=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{Q}{R}$

Substitute

$Q=\pi\rho_0R^3$

Therefore,

$V(R)=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{\pi\rho_0R^3}{R}$

Thus,

$\boxed{V(R)=\frac{\rho_0R^2}{4\varepsilon_0}}$

## Part (h): Potential inside the sphere

The electric field and potential are related by

$V(r)-V(R)=-\int_R^r\vec{E}\cdot d\vec{\ell}$

Inside the sphere, the electric field is radial:

$E(r')=\frac{\rho_0r'^2}{4\varepsilon_0R}$

Therefore,

$V(r)-V(R)=-\int_R^r\frac{\rho_0r'^2}{4\varepsilon_0R},dr'$

Factor out the constants:

$V(r)-V(R)=-\frac{\rho_0}{4\varepsilon_0R}\int_R^r r'^2,dr'$

Evaluating the integral,

$V(r)-V(R)=-\frac{\rho_0}{4\varepsilon_0R}\left(\frac{r^3-R^3}{3}\right)$

Therefore,

$V(r)=V(R)+\frac{\rho_0(R^3-r^3)}{12\varepsilon_0R}$

Using

$V(R)=\frac{\rho_0R^2}{4\varepsilon_0}$

gives

$V(r)=\frac{\rho_0R^2}{4\varepsilon_0}+\frac{\rho_0(R^3-r^3)}{12\varepsilon_0R}$

Writing the first term with a common denominator,

$V(r)=\frac{3\rho_0R^3}{12\varepsilon_0R}+\frac{\rho_0(R^3-r^3)}{12\varepsilon_0R}$

Combining the terms,

$\boxed{V(r)=\frac{\rho_0(4R^3-r^3)}{12\varepsilon_0R}}$

An equivalent form is

$\boxed{V(r)=\frac{\rho_0R^2}{3\varepsilon_0}-\frac{\rho_0r^3}{12\varepsilon_0R}}$

At the center,

$V(0)=\frac{\rho_0R^2}{3\varepsilon_0}$

Therefore,

$\boxed{V(0)=\frac{\rho_0R^2}{3\varepsilon_0}}$

The electric potential is largest at the center and decreases continuously as $r$ increases.

# Complete piecewise results

## Enclosed charge

$Q_{\mathrm{enc}}(r)=\begin{cases}\dfrac{\pi\rho_0r^4}{R},&0\leq r<R,\[6pt]\pi\rho_0R^3,&r\geq R.\end{cases}$

## Electric field

$\vec{E}(r)=\begin{cases}\dfrac{\rho_0r^2}{4\varepsilon_0R}\hat{r},&0\leq r<R,\[8pt]\dfrac{\rho_0R^3}{4\varepsilon_0r^2}\hat{r},&r\geq R.\end{cases}$

## Electric potential

$V(r)=\begin{cases}\dfrac{\rho_0(4R^3-r^3)}{12\varepsilon_0R},&0\leq r<R,\[10pt]\dfrac{\rho_0R^3}{4\varepsilon_0r},&r\geq R.\end{cases}$

# Suggested AP-style scoring guide

## Part (a): 2 points

- 1 point for writing the correct charge integral using the nonuniform density.
- 1 point for obtaining $Q_{\mathrm{enc}}(r)=\dfrac{\pi\rho_0r^4}{R}$.

## Part (b): 2 points

- 1 point for correctly applying Gauss’s law with spherical symmetry.
- 1 point for obtaining $E(r)=\dfrac{\rho_0r^2}{4\varepsilon_0R}$ with the correct direction.

## Part (c): 1 point

- 1 point for obtaining $Q=\pi\rho_0R^3$.

## Part (d): 2 points

- 1 point for using the total enclosed charge outside the sphere.
- 1 point for obtaining $E(r)=\dfrac{\rho_0R^3}{4\varepsilon_0r^2}$.

## Part (e): 1 point

- 1 point for showing that both one-sided field values equal $\dfrac{\rho_0R}{4\varepsilon_0}$.

## Part (f): 2 points

- 1 point for a quadratic increase from zero to the surface.
- 1 point for a continuous $1/r^2$ decrease outside the sphere.

## Part (g): 1 point

- 1 point for obtaining $V(R)=\dfrac{\rho_0R^2}{4\varepsilon_0}$.

## Part (h): 3 points

- 1 point for using the correct potential integral and boundary value.
- 1 point for correctly evaluating the integral.
- 1 point for obtaining an equivalent correct expression for $V(r)$.

## Total: 14 points

# Key AP Physics lessons

1. When $\rho$ varies with position, the enclosed charge must be found by integration.
2. The density at the Gaussian radius cannot simply be multiplied by the entire enclosed volume.
3. Spherical symmetry allows the flux integral to become

$\oint_S\vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A}=E(4\pi r^2)$

1. Inside a distributed charge, use $Q_{\mathrm{enc}}(r)$ rather than the total charge.
2. Outside a spherically symmetric charge distribution, the field is equivalent to that of a point charge containing the total charge.
3. A finite volume charge density does not by itself produce a discontinuity in the electric field.
4. Electric potential is continuous even when the electric field changes its functional form.
5. The electric field is related to the slope of the potential:

$E_r=-\frac{dV}{dr}$

1. The maximum electric field does not always occur where the charge density is greatest; it must be determined from the complete field expression.
2. Every final vector answer should include the electric-field direction.

## One-sentence solution summary

For the nonuniform density $\rho(r)=\rho_0r/R$, the enclosed charge grows as $r^4$, the internal electric field grows as $r^2$, and the external electric field decreases as $1/r^2$.

documents.content:
# AP Statistics FRQ Practice Pack

**Regenerated edition** - topic coverage retained; layout reorganized by difficulty; author/publisher branding removed.

> Study use only. Problem contexts follow publicly released AP Statistics FRQ templates. Practice with hints/templates; do not use on graded work unless allowed.

## How this pack is organized

1. **Four topics kept intact** (Exploring Data → Collecting Data → Probability & RVs → Inference).
2. **Within each topic, problems are ordered by difficulty**:
   - Level 1 Foundation — identify / interpret / short explanation
   - Level 2 Standard — calculation + justification in context
   - Level 3 Challenge — full multi-step inference or layered probability
3. **Substantive stems unchanged** (cleaned for OCR/layout only).
4. **Part II** contains **reference answers** for every problem (worked solutions for self-check).

## Topic map (CED alignment)

| Topic | CED units | Typical FRQ skills |
|---|---|---|
| 1 Exploring Data | Units 1-2 | SOCS, outliers, regression interpret, residuals, r² |
| 2 Collecting Data | Unit 3 | Sampling bias, SRS/stratify, experiments, confounding |
| 3 Probability & RVs | Units 4-5 | Conditional probability, Binomial/Geometric/Normal, EV |
| 4 Statistical Inference | Units 6-9 | CI/tests for p & mu, chi^2, slope inference |

---

# Part I - Practice Problems

## Topic 1 Exploring Data

### T1-F1 · Foundation (Level 1) · Scatterplot language & residual

*Template source label (for study tracking): 2017 FRQ1 (wolves)*

Researchers studying a pack of gray wolves collected data on length x (meters, nose to tip of tail) and weight y (kilograms). A scatterplot of weight versus length was described as positive, linear, and strong.

(a) Explain what is meant by each of the following for this situation:
(i) Positive
(ii) Linear
(iii) Strong

The least-squares equation is y-hat = -16.46 + 35.02x.

(b) Interpret the slope of the least-squares regression line in context.

(c) One wolf with length 1.4 m had residual -9.67 kg. What was the weight of the wolf?

---

### T1-F2 · Foundation (Level 1) · Two-way table proportions & association

*Template source label (for study tracking): 2014 FRQ1 (extracurricular)*

An administrator sampled 100 students. Residential status (on/off campus) and extracurricular participation are summarized:

| Participation | On campus | Off campus | Total |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| No activities | 9 | 30 | 39 |
| One activity | 17 | 25 | 42 |
| Two or more | 7 | 12 | 19 |
| Total | 33 | 67 | 100 |

(a) Calculate the proportion of on-campus students who participate in no extracurricular activity, and the same proportion for off-campus students.

(b) Using the segmented bar graph idea (relative frequencies within each residential status), write a few sentences summarizing the association between residential status and level of participation in the sample.

---

### T1-S1 · Standard (Level 2) · Five-number summary & two outlier rules

*Template source label (for study tracking): 2021 FRQ1 (hospital stay)*

Length of stay (days) for a random sample of 50 patients:

| Days | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 | 9 | 12 | 21 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Patients | 4 | 13 | 14 | 11 | 6 | 1 | 1 |

(a) Determine the five-number summary of the distribution of length of stay.

(b) Method A = 1.5 x IQR rule; Method B = 2 standard deviations from the mean.
(i) Using Method A, identify potential outliers. Justify.
